# ai continual learning

| # | Experiment | Reach | Novelty | Falsifiable | Total |

| 1 | [003-experiment.txt](003-experiment.txt) | 0.78 | 0.74 | 0.69 | 2.21 |

| 10 | [007-experiment.txt](007-experiment.txt) | 0.72 | 0.78 | 0.41 | 1.91 |

| 19 | [020-experiment.txt](020-experiment.txt) | 0.62 | 0.71 | 0.18 | 1.51 |
| 20 | [018-experiment.txt](018-experiment.txt) | 0.31 | 0.62 | 0.18 | 1.11 |

## Top 5

**1. [003-experiment.txt](003-experiment.txt)**  
Thought experiment: absurd priors as learning engines; critique: it conflates memory retention with transfer because “fundamental knowledge” is undefined.

**2. [013-experiment.txt](013-experiment.txt)**  
Thought experiment: selective forgetting and task conflict may enable true learning; critique: undefined jargon makes the claim intriguing but weakly testable.

**3. [001-experiment.txt](001-experiment.txt)**  
Thought experiment: forgetting distills transferable abstractions, so tiny buffers improve continual learning; critique: claim is novel and testable but underspecified.

**4. [006-experiment.txt](006-experiment.txt)**  
Thought experiment: continual learning prevents forgetting by collapsing all novelty into fixed abstractions; critique: elegant but metaphorical, arbitrary, and weakly testable.

**5. [005-experiment.txt](005-experiment.txt)**  
Metaphorically argues continual learning needs selective forgetting amid unstable categories; critique: novel and broad, but too metaphorical to test strongly.
